# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants with two examples each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Primary | Secondary |
> |---|---|---|
> | Definition | Emitted **directly** from source | **Formed in atmosphere** by reactions |
> | Source | Combustion, industries, vehicles | Photochemical & aqueous reactions |
> | Examples | CO, SO₂, NO, PM, VOCs | O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, secondary PM |
> | Control | Easier (source modification) | Difficult (precursor control needed) |
>
> *(2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for examples)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Convert 350 µg/m³ of SO₂ to ppm at 25 °C, 1 atm. (M_w SO₂ = 64 g/mol)**

> **Model Answer:**
> Apply F2:
>
> $$C_{ppm} = \frac{350 \times 24.45}{64} = \frac{8557.5}{64} = 133.7\ \text{ppm}$$
>
> *(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]* ⭐

**(a)** Describe the **classification of air pollutants** based on (i) formation mechanism, (ii) physical state, and (iii) chemical nature. Give at least two examples under each category. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A CPCB monitoring station reports the following 24-hr average concentrations:
- PM₂.₅ = 65 µg/m³
- PM₁₀ = 110 µg/m³
- SO₂ = 35 µg/m³
- NO₂ = 50 µg/m³
- CO = 2.5 mg/m³
- O₃ = 80 µg/m³

**Calculate the National AQI** and identify the dominant pollutant. **Use standard CPCB break-points.** **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

> **Formation-based classification (2 marks)**
> - Primary: emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NO, PM, VOCs)
> - Secondary: formed in atmosphere (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, NO₂)
>
> **Physical state classification (2 marks)**
> - Gaseous: SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃
> - Particulate (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, ultrafine), aerosols, fumes, mist
>
> **Chemical classification (2 marks)**
> - Organic: VOCs, PAHs, aldehydes
> - Inorganic: SO₂, NOₓ, CO, H₂S
>
> **Conclusion with significance (1 mark)**
> Knowledge of classification guides control strategy and standard selection.

#### **Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 — Use CPCB break-point table for each pollutant and compute sub-index using F9:**
>
> | Pollutant | Conc. | BP range | $I_{\text{lo}}$ | $I_{\text{hi}}$ | BP_lo | BP_hi | Sub-index $I_p$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | PM₂.₅ | 65 | 61–90 | 101 | 200 | 61 | 90 | $\frac{99}{29}\times4+101 = 114.66$ |
> | PM₁₀ | 110 | 101–250 | 101 | 200 | 101 | 250 | $\frac{99}{149}\times9+101 = 106.98$ |
> | SO₂ | 35 | 31–60 | 51 | 100 | 31 | 60 | $\frac{49}{29}\times4+51 = 57.76$ |
> | NO₂ | 50 | 41–80 | 51 | 100 | 41 | 80 | $\frac{49}{39}\times9+51 = 62.31$ |
> | CO | 2.5 | 2.1–10 mg/m³ | 51 | 100 | 2.1 | 10 | $\frac{49}{7.9}\times0.4+51 = 53.48$ |
> | O₃ | 80 | 58–108 | 51 | 100 | 58 | 108 | $\frac{49}{50}\times22+51 = 72.56$ |
>
> **Step 2 — Overall AQI = Maximum sub-index** = **114.66 (PM₂.₅)**.
>
> **Step 3 — AQI Category:** Since 101–200 = **"Poor"** (CPCB category), the dominant pollutant is **PM₂.₅**.
>
> **Conclusion:** The city falls in the "Poor" AQI band; immediate PM₂.₅ mitigation measures are required.

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* ⭐

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **EPA Method 5 stack-sampling train** for the determination of particulate matter emissions. List the function of each component. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 1.5 m diameter stack at a cement plant has a flue gas temperature of 180 °C, pressure 1 atm, and the gas composition (by mole) is 12% CO₂, 6% H₂O, 4% O₂, and 78% N₂. The average velocity measured by a Type-S Pitot tube is 14 m/s. Calculate the **actual volumetric flow rate (m³/s)**, the **density of flue gas**, and the **mass flow rate (kg/s)**, assuming ideal-gas behaviour. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

> **Diagram (4 marks)** — draw labelled schematic of the EPA Method 5 train as in Section 4.2.
>
> **Component functions (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Nozzle** — provides isokinetic sampling entry.
> 2. **Type-S Pitot tube** — measures velocity pressure (ΔH).
> 3. **Heated probe** (~120 °C) — prevents condensation of moisture/sulphuric acid mist.
> 4. **Filter holder + glass-fibre filter** — captures particulate matter for gravimetric analysis.
> 5. **Condenser / impinger train** — absorbs gaseous pollutants (e.g., SO₂ in H₂O₂, HCl in water).
> 6. **Drying tube + silica gel** — removes residual moisture before metering.
> 7. **Meter box** — measures total gas volume sampled.
> 8. **Vacuum pump** — draws sample through the train.

#### **Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

> **Step 1 — Average molecular weight of flue gas (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$M_w = 0.12(44) + 0.06(18) + 0.04(32) + 0.78(28)$$
> $$= 5.28 + 1.08 + 1.28 + 21.84 = 29.48\ g/mol = 0.02948\ kg/mol$$
>
> **Step 2 — Density of flue gas (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$T = 180 + 273 = 453\ K,\quad P = 101325\ Pa$$
> $$\rho_g = \frac{P M_w}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 0.02948}{8.314 \times 453} = 0.793\ kg/m^3$$
>
> **Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of stack (1 mark):**
>
> $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^2 = 1.767\ m^2$$
>
> **Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate at stack conditions (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$Q_{\text{actual}} = A \cdot v_s = 1.767 \times 14 = 24.74\ m^3/s$$
>
> **Step 5 — Mass flow rate (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$\dot{m} = \rho_g \cdot Q = 0.793 \times 24.74 = 19.62\ kg/s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — DO NOT LOSE THESE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Unit errors** in ppm ↔ µg/m³ conversion: most students forget the factor **24.45** — write it on the formula sheet and use it.
> 2. **Temperature must be in Kelvin** (°C + 273). Converting incorrectly gives wrong density and wrong AQI.
> 3. **AQI = MAX, not average** — examiners explicitly deduct if a student writes the average.
> 4. **CPCB break-points are different for CO (mg/m³) vs SO₂ (µg/m³)** — re-check units before interpolation.
> 5. **Pitot tube constant** is **0.84 for Type-S**; using 1.0 loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Isokinetic sampling** is a *must-mention* concept when describing stack sampling.
> 7. **EPA Method 5** requires the filter to be dried & weighed pre/post to ±0.1 mg — state the gravimetric principle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Air Pollution Basics**
> - Definition: presence of contaminants causing measurable harm (Indian Air Act, 1981).
> - Analogy: atmosphere = cafeteria soup bowl; ppm = "pinch of spice in 1000 L".

> ✅ **Units of Measurement**
> - Volume-ratio: **ppm = 10⁻⁶, ppb = 10⁻⁹, % = 10⁻²**
> - Mass: **µg/m³, mg/m³, ng/m³**
> - Emission: **kg/hr, t/day, Nm³/hr**
> - **Master formula**: $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times M_w \times 40.87$ (at 25 °C, 1 atm).

> ✅ **Sources**
> - **Natural**: volcanic, forest fires, dust, biogenic, lightning.
> - **Anthropogenic**: stationary, mobile, area, line.

> ✅ **Classification**
> - Primary vs Secondary
> - Gaseous vs Particulate
> - Organic vs Inorganic
> - Criteria pollutants (PM, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb)
> - Hazardous Air Pollutants (189 listed by USEPA)

> ✅ **Sampling**
> - **Ambient**: High-Vol Sampler (PM₁₀), EPA FRM impactor (PM₂.₅), impinger trains (SO₂, NO₂).
> - **Stack**: EPA Method 5 train (Nozzle → Pitot → Heated Probe → Filter → Condenser → Impingers → Dryer → Meter → Pump).
> - **Isokinetic** sampling mandatory for accurate PM measurement.

> ✅ **Analysis**
> - **Gravimetric** for PM
> - **West-Gaeke** (p-Rosaniline) for SO₂
> - **Griess-Saltzman** for NOₓ
> - **NDIR** for CO
> - **UV Photometric** for O₃
> - **AAS/ICP-MS** for Pb
> - **GC-MS / GC-FID** for VOCs

> ✅ **AQI — Must Memorise**
> - Formula: linear interpolation F9.
> - **AQI = MAX of all sub-indices.**
> - Bands: Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500).

> ✅ **Key Pitfalls**
> - Always Kelvin for temperature in gas equations.
> - M_w must be in **g/mol** for ppm conversion.
> - Stack gas velocity via Pitot tube: $v_s = 0.84\sqrt{2 g \Delta H \cdot \rho_w / \rho_g}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health effects of air pollution, global warming, and ozone depletion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Health effects of air pollution, global warming, and ozone depletion ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Air Pollution — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** *Air pollution* refers to the presence of one or more contaminants in the atmosphere in quantities and duration that are injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, or property, or which unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or the conduct of business. Air pollution is quantified by **concentration (µg/m³ or ppm)**, **duration of exposure**, and the **toxicity index** of each pollutant. The **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)** specify that **PM₂.₅ annual mean should not exceed 5 µg/m³** and **PM₁₀ should not exceed 15 µg/m³**. ### 1.2 The Six Classical "Criteria Pollutants" | # | Pollutant | Primary Sources | Key Health Endpoint | |---|-----------|-----------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅)** | Combustion, dust, vehicular | Deep lung penetration, alveolar damage | | 2 | **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** | Coal/oil combustion, smelters | Bronchoconstriction, asthma | | 3 | **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** | Vehicular exhaust, power plants | Pulmonary edema, smog formation | | 4 | **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | Incomplete combustion | Hemoglobin binding (carboxyhemoglobin) | | 5 | **Ground-Level Ozone (O₃)** | Photochemical smog | Lung inflammation, crop damage | | 6 | **Lead (Pb)** | Leaded fuel, smelters | Neurotoxicity in children | ### 1.3 Global Warming — The Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Greenhouse Effect Analogy:** Imagine the Earth wrapped in a *transparent thermal blanket*. Short-wave solar radiation passes through easily, but the *long-wave infrared radiation* emitted by the warm Earth's surface gets *absorbed and re-radiated* by trace gases — analogous to a one-way mirror. The more blanket (GHG concentration), the warmer the interior (troposphere). ### 1.4 Ozone Depletion — The Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stratospheric Ozone — The UV Shield Analogy:** Stratospheric ozone (O₃) at 15–35 km altitude acts as Earth's *sunscreen*, absorbing harmful Ultraviolet-B (UV-B, 280–315 nm) radiation. Chlorine radicals (Cl•) released from CFCs act like *molecular scissors* — one Cl atom can destroy **~100,000 ozone molecules** in a catalytic chain (the *Chapman–Molina cycle*). ### 1.5 Real-World Damage Metrics - **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year):** One lost year of "healthy" life. Used by WHO to quantify disease burden. - **Mortality Rate per 100,000:** Standard epidemiological metric. - **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP):** Relative destructive capacity vs. CFC-11 (= 1.0). - **Global Warming Potential (GWP):** Relative heat-trapping capacity vs. CO₂ (= 1.0) over a time horizon (typically 100 years). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Health Effects Pathway Human health impact follows this sequential causal chain: 1. **Emission Source** → releases contaminant 2. **Atmospheric Transport & Transformation** → secondary pollutant formation (e.g., NO₂ + VOC + sunlight → O₃) 3. **Inhalation / Dermal Contact / Ingestion** → dose enters body 4. **Biological Dose at Target Organ** → deposition in alveoli, blood–gas barrier 5. **Biochemical Response** → inflammation, oxidative stress, enzyme inhibition 6. **Clinical Outcome** → asthma, COPD, cancer, death ### 2.2 Dose–Response for Carbon Monoxide The **Coburn–Forster–Kane (CFK) equation** predicts the rate of carboxyhemoglobin (COHb%) formation in blood: $$ \frac{d[COHb]}{dt} = \frac{V_{CO} - \frac{[COHb]\cdot V_{CO}\cdot P_{CO}}{P_{CO} \cdot M}}{V_{b}\cdot Hb} $$ where: - $V_{CO}$ = CO generation rate (mL/min) - $V_b$ = blood volume (mL) - $Hb$ = hemoglobin concentration - $P_{CO}$ = partial pressure of CO - $M$ = Haldane constant ≈ **218** ### 2.3 Global Warming — Radiative Forcing The change in outgoing long-wave radiation per unit change in CO₂ concentration (Myhre et al., 1998): $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_{0}}\right) \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$ where: - $C$ = current CO₂ concentration (ppm) - $C_0$ = reference concentration (pre-industrial ≈ 280 ppm) - $\Delta F$ = radiative forcing change > [!NOTE] > **Why the logarithm?** CO₂ absorption bands saturate. Doubling the gas does *not* double the warming — only adds another fixed increment. ### 2.4 Stefan–Boltzmann Equilibrium Temperature $$ E = \sigma T^{4} $$ with **σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴** (Stefan–Boltzmann constant). Equilibrium Earth surface temperature accounting for greenhouse effect: $$ T_{s} = \left(\frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4\sigma(1-\frac{\epsilon}{2})}\right)^{1/4} $$ where: - $S$ = solar constant ≈ **1361 W/m²** - $\alpha$ = albedo ≈ **0.30** - $\epsilon$ = effective emissivity of atmosphere ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table) | Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | |---------|-----------|-------------------| | Stefan–Boltzmann | $E = \sigma T^{4}$ | E = W/m², σ = 5.67×10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴, T = K | | Radiative Forcing (CO₂) | $\Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln(C/C_{0})$ | F = W/m², C in ppm | | GWP | $GWP_{x} = \frac{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{x}\cdot c_{x}(t)\, dt}{\int_{0}^{TH} a_{CO_{2}}\cdot c_{CO_{2}}(t)\, dt}$ | TH = time horizon (yrs), a = radiative efficiency | | ODP | $ODP_{x} = \frac{\text{Cl atoms released by } x}{\text{Cl atoms released by CFC-11}}$ | dimensionless | | CFK equation | $\frac{d[COHb]}{dt} = \frac{V_{CO}}{V_b\cdot Hb}\left[1 - \frac{[COHb]}{100\cdot M}\right]$ | COHb in % | | DALY | $DALY = YLL + YLD$ | Years of life lost + years lived with disability | | Clausius–Clapeyron (water-holding capacity) | $e_{s} = 6.112 \cdot e^{\frac{17.67\,T}{T+243.5}}$ | T in °C, eₛ in hPa | | Climate Sensitivity | $\Delta T_{2\times} = \lambda \cdot \Delta F_{2\times}$ | λ = 0.8 K/(W/m²), ΔF₂ₓ ≈ 3.7 W/m² | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility - **Process Designers** use CFK equation to design ventilation systems in tunnels, parking garages, and steel plants. - **Emission Inventory Engineers** compute CO₂-equivalent emissions as: $E_{CO_{2}-eq} = \sum_i (m_i \cdot GWP_i)$. - **Refrigeration Engineers** select refrigerants based on ODP and GWP — the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes this in *Green Refrigeration*. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Doubling CO₂ Forcing from Pre-Industrial **Problem:** Calculate the radiative forcing if atmospheric CO₂ rises from **280 ppm (1750)** to **560 ppm (doubled)**. **Step 1 — Identify input variables** $$ C_0 = 280 \text{ ppm}, \quad C = 560 \text{ ppm} $$ **Step 2 — Form ratio** $$ \frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{560}{280} = 2.0 $$ **Step 3 — Apply Myhre logarithmic relationship** $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \cdot \ln(2.0) $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate natural log** $$ \ln(2.0) = 0.6931 $$ **Step 5 — Multiply by 5.35** $$ \Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.6931 = 3.708 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 6 — Result** $$ \boxed{\Delta F_{2\times CO_2} \approx 3.7 \text{ W/m}^2} $$ This is the **canonical IPCC value** used universally in climate modelling. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Equilibrium Surface Temperature **Given:** $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$, $\epsilon = 0.77$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ **Step 1 — Solar absorbed** $$ F_{abs} = \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} = \frac{1361 \times 0.70}{4} = 238.2 \text{ W/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Effective emissivity factor** $$ 1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2} = 1 - 0.385 = 0.615 $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ T_s = \left(\frac{238.2}{5.67\times10^{-8} \times 0.615}\right)^{1/4} $$ **Step 4 — Denominator** $$ 5.67\times10^{-8} \times 0.615 = 3.487\times10^{-8} $$ **Step 5 — Division** $$ \frac{238.2}{3.487\times10^{-8}} = 6.83\times10^{9} $$ **Step 6 — Fourth root** $$ T_s = (6.83\times10^{9})^{0.25} \approx 288.6 \text{ K} \approx 15.5\,^\circ\text{C} $$ **Result:** Earth mean surface temperature ≈ **288 K** (matches observed 288 K — proof of model validity). --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Carbon Monoxide Steady-State COHb% **Scenario:** Garage worker, ambient CO = **50 ppm** for 8 hours. Compute equilibrium COHb%. **Step 1 — Use simplified empirical relation (Coburn et al.):** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{643}{[CO]_{ppm}}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute [CO] = 50 ppm** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{643}{50}} = \frac{1}{1 + 12.86} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify** $$ [COHb]_{ss}\% = \frac{1}{13.86} = 0.0721 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to percent** $$ \boxed{[COHb]_{ss} \approx 7.2\%} $$ **Interpretation:** OSHA permissible exposure limit (PEL) for CO is **50 ppm** → produces ~7% COHb — *just below the 10% clinical threshold for headache and dizziness*. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant vs. Health Endpoint | Pollutant | Primary Health Effect | Dose Metric | Critical Receptor | |-----------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------| | **PM₂.₅** | Cardiovascular mortality, lung cancer | µg/m³ (annual mean) | Alveolar macrophages | | **PM₁₀** | Asthma exacerbation, bronchitis | µg/m³ (24-hr) | Bronchial epithelium | | **SO₂** | Bronchoconstriction (5-min avg) | ppb | Smooth muscle | | **NO₂** | Pulmonary inflammation | µg/m³ | Type-II pneumocytes | | **CO** | Tissue hypoxia | %COHb | Hemoglobin (Fe²⁺) | | **O₃** | Reduced lung function (FEV₁) | 8-hr max, ppb | Centriacinar region | | **Pb** | IQ deficit in children | µg/dL blood | Neuro-developmental | | **C₆H₆** | Leukemia | ppm-yr cumulative | Bone marrow | | **HCHO** | Sensory irritation | ppb | Mucous membranes | | **PAH (BaP)** | Lung/skin cancer | ng/m³ | DNA adducts | --- ### 3.5 Derivation: GWP of Methane over 100 Years **Given:** Radiative efficiencies, $a_{CH_4} = 1.45 \times 10^{-13}$ W·m⁻²·kg⁻¹, $a_{CO_2} = 1.76 \times 10^{-15}$ W·m⁻²·kg⁻¹. Methane atmospheric lifetime ≈ **12 years**; CO₂ lifetime ≈ **infinite (long tail)**. **Step 1 — Time-integrated decay (simplified exponential):** $$ \int_0^{TH} e^{-t/\tau}\, dt = \tau\left(1 - e^{-TH/\tau}\right) $$ For CH₄, τ = 12 yr, TH = 100 yr: $$ 12 \times \left(1 - e^{-100/12}\right) = 12 \times (1 - 2.46\times10^{-4}) \approx 11.997 $$ For CO₂ (impulse-response function — simplified): $$ \int_0^{100} f_{CO_2}(t)\, dt \approx 0.30 \text{ (dimensionless)} $$ **Step 2 — Apply GWP formula** $$ GWP_{CH_4} = \frac{a_{CH_4}}{a_{CO_2}} \cdot \frac{\int f_{CH_4}\, dt}{\int f_{CO_2}\, dt} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $$ GWP_{CH_4} = \frac{1.45\times10^{-13}}{1.76\times10^{-15}} \cdot \frac{11.997}{0.30} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate ratios** $$ 82.39 \times 39.99 = 3294 $$ **Step 5 — Apply IPCC AR5 correction for climate-carbon feedback** $$ \boxed{GWP_{100,\,CH_4} \approx 28{-}36 \text{ (commonly cited as 28)}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners expect **28** (without feedback) or **34** (with feedback) as standard answers. Memorize both. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Causal Pathway: Emission → Health Outcome ```mermaid flowchart TD A[EMISSION SOURCE
Power Plant, Vehicle, Industry] B[ATMOSPHERIC DISPERSION
Wind, Stability, Topography] C[TRANSFORMATION
Photochemistry, Secondary PM] D[EXPOSURE
Inhalation, Dermal, Ingestion] E[BIOLOGICAL DOSE
Lung Deposition, Blood-Brain Transfer] F[BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE
Oxidative Stress, Inflammation] G[CLINICAL EFFECT
Asthma, COPD, Cancer, Death] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cca500,color:#000000 style D fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style E fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000000 style F fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1155cc,color:#000000 style G fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.2 Ozone Depletion — Chapman–Molina Catalytic Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR CFC[CFC
CF2Cl2] UV1[UV PHOTON
Lambda less than 220 nm] ClRad[Cl RADICAL
Chlorine Atom] O3M[O3 MOLECULE
Stratospheric Ozone] ClO[ClO INTERMEDIATE] O2M[O2 MOLECULE
Diatomic Oxygen] O_ATOM[O ATOM
Free Oxygen] ClRecycle[Cl RECYCLES
Chain Reaction] CFC -->|UV breaks bond| UV1 UV1 -->|Releases| ClRad ClRad -->|Attacks| O3M O3M -->|Forms| ClO ClO -->|Reacts with| O_ATOM O_ATOM -->|Releases| ClRecycle ClRecycle -->|Reattacks| O3M O3M -.->|Net loss| O2M style CFC fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 style ClRad fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff style O3M fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style ClRecycle fill:#ff6666,stroke:#cc0000,color:#ffffff style O2M fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Greenhouse Effect — Energy Balance Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SUN[SUN
Solar Constant S equals 1361 W per sq m] SW[SHORTWAVE RADIATION
UV and Visible] SURF[EARTH SURFACE
Albedo 0.30, Absorbs 70 percent] LW[LONGWAVE INFRARED
Re-emitted by Earth] GH[GREEHOUSE GASES
CO2, CH4, N2O, H2O, O3] TR[TROPOSPHERE
Warming of Lower Atmosphere] SPACE[OUTER SPACE
Effective radiating altitude] SUN --> SW SW -->|Net absorbed| SURF SURF -->|Emits| LW LW -->|Partially absorbed| GH GH -->|Re-radiates both directions| TR GH -->|Rest escapes to| SPACE style SUN fill:#ffe599,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style SURF fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#1c4587,color:#ffffff style GH fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000,color:#ffffff style TR fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.4 Global Warming Potential — Comparative Block ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TimeHorizon[GWP OVER 100 YEARS] CO2[CO2
GWP equals 1
Reference Gas] CH4[CH4
GWP equals 28
Methane] N2O[N2O
GWP equals 265
Nitrous Oxide] HFC[HFC
GWP equals 1300
Hydrofluorocarbon] SF6[SF6
GWP equals 23500
Sulphur Hexafluoride] end CO2 --- CH4 CH4 --- N2O N2O --- HFC HFC --- SF6 style CO2 fill:#cccccc,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 style CH4 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000000 style N2O fill:#ffd966,stroke:#cc8400,color:#000000 style HFC fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000 style SF6 fill:#990000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Differentiate between **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** and **GWP (Global Warming Potential)**. Why is ODP of CFC-11 taken as the reference value? > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - **ODP** = relative capacity of a substance to destroy stratospheric ozone, referenced to CFC-11 (ODP = 1.0) because CFC-11 was the most prolific early ozone-depleting substance, was widely measured, and has a well-defined atmospheric lifetime of ~45 years. > - **GWP** = relative radiative forcing impact of a greenhouse gas over a chosen time horizon (typically 100 years), referenced to CO₂ (GWP = 1.0) because CO₂ is the most abundant anthropogenic GHG. > - Key distinction: ODP is **stratospheric chemistry** (Cl-catalysed O₃ destruction); GWP is **tropospheric radiative physics** (IR absorption). *(1 mark for each distinction + 1 mark for CFC-11 rationale.)* **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` Explain why **PM₂.₅ is more hazardous to human health than PM₁₀**. Cite two specific disease endpoints. > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - PM₂.₅ has aerodynamic diameter ≤ 2.5 µm, allowing it to bypass upper-airway filtration and reach **alveolar sacs** of the lungs, whereas PM₁₀ is largely trapped in bronchioles. *(1 mark)* > - PM₂.₅ enters systemic circulation causing **cardiovascular mortality** (atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction). *(1 mark)* > - PM₂.₅ also induces **lung adenocarcinoma** via oxidative DNA damage in alveolar epithelium. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Do NOT skip units** in numerical answers — losing 1 mark per numerical. > - **Memorize GWP values** for CO₂ (=1), CH₄ (=28), N₂O (=265) — KTU examiners frequently test these in 2-mark sub-parts. > - **Always state assumptions** in derivation questions (e.g., "assuming well-mixed atmosphere", "assuming steady state"). > - In 14-mark questions, present **diagrams/flowcharts wherever possible** — adds 1–2 marks in impression scoring. > - For health-effect questions, **name specific diseases** (asthma, COPD, mesothelioma) rather than vague terms like "lung problems". --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** With the help of the Stefan–Boltzmann law, derive the expression for **equilibrium surface temperature** of the Earth, given effective atmospheric emissivity $\epsilon$. State the values of solar constant, albedo, and Stefan–Boltzmann constant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the Myhre logarithmic relationship, compute the **radiative forcing** when atmospheric CO₂ rises from 280 ppm to **420 ppm**. Also calculate the **equilibrium temperature increase** using climate sensitivity $\lambda = 0.8$ K/(W/m²). **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution (a):** > > **Step 1 — Incoming Solar Flux at Earth's Surface (averaged over sphere):** > $$ > F_{in} = \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} > $$ > $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$ *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Outgoing Long-wave Radiation (OLR):** > $$ > F_{out} = \epsilon \sigma T_{s}^{4} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Effective emissivity with single-layer atmosphere:** > Atmosphere absorbs $\epsilon \sigma T_s^4$ and re-emits $\epsilon \sigma T_a^4$ both up and down. With $T_a < T_s$, net outgoing becomes: > $$ > F_{out,\,net} = \frac{\epsilon \sigma T_s^4}{2} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Equate absorbed to emitted:** > $$ > \frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4} = \epsilon \sigma T_s^4 \left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\right) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Solve for Tₛ:** > $$ > T_s = \left[\frac{S(1-\alpha)}{4\epsilon\sigma\left(1 - \frac{\epsilon}{2}\right)}\right]^{1/4} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Constants box:** > $S = 1361$ W/m², $\alpha = 0.30$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Numerical check:** > With $\epsilon = 0.77$, $T_s \approx 288$ K, matches observation. *(1 mark)* > **Model Solution (b):** > > **Step 1 — Write Myhre formula:** > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Plug values:** > $C_0 = 280$, $C = 420$: > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \ln\left(\frac{420}{280}\right) > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Compute ratio:** > $$ > \frac{420}{280} = 1.5 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Compute log:** > $$ > \ln(1.5) = 0.4055 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Multiply:** > $$ > \Delta F = 5.35 \times 0.4055 = 2.169 \text{ W/m}^2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Temperature rise:** > $$ > \Delta T = \lambda \times \Delta F = 0.8 \times 2.169 = 1.735 \text{ K} \approx 1.74\,^\circ\text{C} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Real-world significance:** Matches IPCC observation that 420 ppm CO₂ corresponds to ~1.1–1.3 °C warming above pre-industrial. *(1 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion** by CFCs using the Chapman–Molina catalytic cycle. Identify the role of polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs) in the Antarctic ozone hole. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)**. A study attributes **120 deaths** in a city of 5 million to PM₂.₅ exposure, with each victim losing an average of **8 years** of life. Additionally, **40,000 cases of chronic bronchitis** were reported, each causing disability weight 0.2 for **10 years**. Calculate total DALYs and interpret the result. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution (a):** > > **Step 1 — CFCs released at ground rise to stratosphere** (lifetimes 50–100 years). *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — UV photolysis (λ < 220 nm) breaks C–Cl bond:** > $$ > \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Cl radical destroys ozone:** > $$ > \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — ClO reacts with free O atom:** > $$ > \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Net reaction:** > $$ > \text{O} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{O}_2 > $$ > Cl regenerated → **one Cl destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules**. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — PSC role:** > Polar Stratospheric Clouds at –78 °C provide surfaces for heterogeneous reactions: > $$ > \text{ClONO}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{PSC surface}} \text{Cl}_2 + \text{HNO}_3 > $$ > They *activate* chlorine from stable reservoirs. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Antarctic vortex:** > Winter isolation allows Cl₂ accumulation; spring sunlight triggers massive O₃ destruction — **Antarctic Ozone Hole**. *(1 mark)* > **Model Solution (b):** > > **Step 1 — Define DALY:** > $$ > \text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD} > $$ > where YLL = Years of Life Lost due to premature death; YLD = Years Lived with Disability. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 2 — Compute YLL:** > $$ > \text{YLL} = 120 \text{ deaths} \times 8 \text{ years} = 960 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 3 — Compute YLD:** > $$ > \text{YLD} = 40{,}000 \times 0.2 \times 10 = 80{,}000 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 4 — Total DALY:** > $$ > \text{DALY}_{total} = 960 + 80{,}000 = 80{,}960 \text{ years} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 5 — Per-capita burden:** > $$ > \frac{80{,}960}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.0162 \text{ DALYs/person/year} > $$ > *(1 mark)* > > **Step 6 — Interpretation:** Chronic bronchitis (YLD) contributes **98.8%** of burden — far outweighing mortality (YLL = 1.2%). Public-health strategy must prioritize **chronic morbidity reduction**, not just mortality. *(1 mark)* > > **Step 7 — Policy relevance:** WHO classifies >0.005 DALYs/person as *significant*; 0.0162 indicates **severe** pollution-related health burden. *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Six criteria pollutants**: PM, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb — know sources, sinks, and health endpoints. - ⭐ **PM₂.₅ vs PM₁₀**: PM₂.₅ penetrates alveoli → systemic → cardiovascular mortality. - ⭐ **Coburn–Forster–Kane equation** is the KTU-favoured model for COHb build-up. - ⭐ **Greenhouse gases in priority order by GWP**: SF₆ (23,500) > HFCs (1000–12000) > N₂O (265) > CH₄ (28) > CO₂ (1). - ⭐ **Stefan–Boltzmann constant σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** — must be memorized. - ⭐ **Myhre logarithmic forcing** formula: $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0)$ — yields **3.7 W/m²** for CO₂ doubling. - ⭐ **Climate sensitivity λ ≈ 0.8 K/(W/m²)** → doubling CO₂ gives **~3 K warming**. - ⭐ **Chapman–Molina cycle** — one Cl destroys ~100,000 O₃ molecules; CFCs are the primary source. - ⭐ **PSCs** activate chlorine → Antarctic Ozone Hole (every September since 1980s). - ⭐ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs; recovery expected by **~2066**. - ⭐ **DALY = YLL + YLD** — distinguish mortality burden from disability burden. - ⭐ **Dobson Units (DU)** measure stratospheric ozone; 220 DU is the "ozone hole" threshold. - ⭐ **WHO 2021 PM₂.₅ guideline: 5 µg/m³ annual, 15 µg/m³ for PM₁₀**. - ⭐ **CFC-11 reference**: ODP = 1.0; **CO₂ reference**: GWP = 1.0. - ⭐ **Albedo α ≈ 0.30**, **Solar constant S ≈ 1361 W/m²** — KTU examiners test these constants directly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always **diagram your answer** for 14-mark questions. A neatly-labelled flowchart of the *emission-to-disease pathway* or the *Chapman–Molina cycle* instantly earns **1–2 impression marks** and ensures full credit on structure-based rubrics. End every numerical with a **sanity-check sentence** interpreting the result in real-world terms.

topics.title: Devices for particulate contaminants: gravitational settling, centrifugal collectors, wet collectors, fabric filters, and electrostatic precipitators (ESP)
Control devices for gaseous contaminants: adsorption, absorption, condensation, combustion, automobile emission control
Odour pollution management: sources, health impacts, measurement and monitoring, control methods
Generic water management techniques
Pollution prevention in the electronic, power generation, electroplating, and fabricated metal products industries
Emerging pollution: threats and pollution caused by biotechnology and nanotechnology
Application of biotechnology and nanotechnology in pollution control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Application of biotechnology and nanotechnology in pollution control # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biotechnology in Pollution Control — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biotechnology in Pollution Control** is the controlled application of biological agents — *microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, algae), plants, enzymes, and their metabolic products* — to **degrade, transform, immobilize, or detoxify** environmental pollutants in soil, water, and air matrices. The branch operating at the industrial scale is formally called **Environmental Biotechnology** or **Bioremediation Engineering**. Sub-disciplines relevant to KTU 2024: | Sub-discipline | Target Pollutant Class | Active Agent | |---|---|---| | **Microbial Bioremediation** | Petroleum hydrocarbons, PAHs, pesticides | *Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Mycobacterium* | | **Phytoremediation** | Heavy metals, radionuclides, organics | Sunflower, *Brassica*, water hyacinth | | **Phycoremediation** | Nutrients (N, P), dyes, CO₂ | Microalgae (*Chlorella*, *Spirulina*) | | **Enzymatic Biodegradation** | Phenols, chlorinated compounds | Laccase, peroxidase, laccase-mediator | | **Biofiltration / Bioscrubbing** | VOCs, H₂S, NH₃ | Biofilms on packing media | ## 1.2 Nanotechnology in Pollution Control — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Nanotechnology in Pollution Control** is the engineering of materials with at least one dimension between **1 nm and 100 nm** to exploit their *quantum-confined, high surface-area, and surface-plasmon* properties for the **detection, capture, catalytic degradation, and sensing** of pollutants. The discipline is termed **Environmental Nanotechnology** or **Nano-remediation**. Key nanomaterial families: | Nanomaterial | Formula / Type | Primary Application | |---|---|---| | **Titanium dioxide (TiO₂)** | Semiconductor photocatalyst | UV-driven AOP for organics | | **Zero-valent Iron (nZVI)** | Fe⁰ core-shell | Reductive dechlorination, Cr(VI) reduction | | **Carbon nanotubes (CNT)** | Single/Multi-walled | Adsorption of dyes, heavy metals | | **Graphene oxide (GO)** | 2-D sp² carbon | Membrane fabrication, adsorbent | | **Magnetic nanoparticles (MNP)** | Fe₃O₄, γ-Fe₂O₃ | Targeted recovery, sludge separation | | **Nanosensors** | Au-NPs, QDs | Real-time Hg²⁺, pesticide, BOD detection | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — Biotechnology:** Imagine a contaminated river as a "kitchen after a party." Microorganisms are *specialized cleaning crews* you hire. Some crews (aerobic bacteria) need oxygen and eat oil spills; some (anaerobic) work in dark, oxygen-free zones and break down chlorinated solvents; *plants* are like floating vacuums absorbing metals through their roots; *algae* are solar-powered nutrient sponges. Biotechnology is simply *recruiting nature's own workforce* and providing them the right working conditions (pH, temperature, electron acceptors). > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — Nanotechnology:** Now take a sugar cube and grind it to 1 billionth-of-a-meter particles. The same mass now has the surface area of a football field. Pollutants are *trapped, stuck, or chemically shredded* on this enormous surface. TiO₂ nanoparticles are like *molecular scissors* — UV light activates them, they generate hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and these radicals rip apart dye molecules and pesticides into CO₂ + H₂O. ## 1.4 Critical Physical Constants & Metrics - **TiO₂ band-gap energy (anatase):** $E_g = 3.2\ \text{eV}$ → activation wavelength $\lambda \leq 388\ \text{nm}$ (UV-A) - **Speed of light:** $c = 3.0 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$ - **Standard surface area of nZVI:** **20–50 m²/g** (vs. 0.05 m²/g for granular iron) - **Definition of nanoscale:** $1\ \text{nm} = 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$ - **Bioremediation cleanup threshold:** residual pollutant $\leq$ **regulatory limit** (e.g., Total Petroleum Hydrocarbons $\leq 0.5\%$ w/w for land farming closure) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Microbial Bioremediation The complete biogeochemical cycle involves five sequential steps: 1. **Bioavailability** — Pollutant must be in the aqueous or vapor phase; hydrophobic compounds (log $K_{ow} > 4$) require **surfactant amendment** (e.g., Tween-80). 2. **Uptake** — Active transport across the cell membrane; large compounds enter via **TOL plasmid**-encoded enzymes. 3. **Catabolism** — Microbial enzymes (oxygenases, dehydrogenases) introduce O₂ or electrons to break bonds. 4. **Energy harvesting** — ATP generation via β-oxidation, TCA cycle. 5. **Mineralization** — End products: CO₂ + H₂O + biomass (aerobic) or CH₄ + CO₂ (anaerobic). ### 2.1.1 Electron Acceptor Hierarchy (Thermodynamic Preference) $$\text{O}_2 > \text{NO}_3^- > \text{Mn}^{4+} > \text{Fe}^{3+} > \text{SO}_4^{2-} > \text{CO}_2$$ **Why this order matters for KTU design:** The engineer must supply the **most energetically favorable acceptor** to maximize degradation rate. O₂ is supplied via **aeration** (typical dissolved oxygen $\geq 2\ \text{mg/L}$ for aerobic treatment). ## 2.2 Phytoremediation Sub-processes | Sub-process | Mechanism | Target Pollutant | |---|---|---| | **Phytoextraction** | Root uptake → translocate → harvest shoot | Cd, Pb, Ni, As | | **Phytodegradation** | Plant enzymes (dehalogenase, nitroreductase) | TCE, atrazine | | **Phytostabilization** | Root immobilization | As, Cr in mine tailings | | **Rhizofiltration** | Adsorption onto roots | Cs, Sr, Cr in water | | **Phytovolatilization** | Release via stomata | Hg → Hg⁰, TCE | **Bioaccumulation factor (BAF):** $$\text{BAF} = \frac{C_{\text{plant}}}{C_{\text{soil/solution}}}$$ **Translocation factor (TF):** $$\text{TF} = \frac{C_{\text{shoot}}}{C_{\text{root}}}$$ > A plant is a "hyperaccumulator" only when **BAF > 1**, **TF > 1**, AND shoot concentration exceeds the **hyperaccumulation threshold** (e.g., > 100 mg/kg for Cd, > 1000 mg/kg for As). ## 2.3 Nanotechnology — Why Nanoparticles Work The **surface-area-to-volume ratio** for a spherical particle of radius $r$ is: $$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$ Reducing $r$ from 1 µm (microparticle) to 10 nm (nanoparticle) gives a **100× increase in SA/V**. This is the *single most important reason* nanomaterials are powerful sorbents and catalysts. ## 2.4 Photocatalysis Mechanism (TiO₂ / UV) A photon of energy $h\nu$ excites an electron from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving a hole ($h^+$). Both species are *powerful redox agents*: $$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \;\longrightarrow\; \text{e}^-_{CB} + \text{h}^+_{VB}$$ $$\text{h}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{H}^+$$ $$\text{e}^- + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{O}_2^{\cdot -}$$ The hydroxyl radical ($\cdot$OH) has a redox potential of **+2.80 V (vs. NHE)** — second only to fluorine — enabling **non-selective mineralization** of organics. ## 2.5 nZVI Reaction with Chlorinated Compounds $$\text{Fe}^0 + \text{R-Cl} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{R-H} + \text{Cl}^-$$ For trichloroethylene (TCE): $$\text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + 3\text{Fe}^0 + 3\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 3\text{Cl}^-$$ Cr(VI) reduction: $$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 2\text{Fe}^0 + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the equations repeatedly tested in KTU ESE.** | # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | KTU Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Surface area to volume ratio (sphere) | $SA/V = 3/r$ | $r$ in m, SA/V in m⁻¹ | Justifying nano-adsorption | | 2 | Pseudo-first-order kinetics | $\ln(C_0/C_t) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t$ | $k_{\text{obs}}$ in min⁻¹, $t$ in min | Photocatalytic degradation | | 3 | Half-life | $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$ | min | Reactor design | | 4 | Langmuir–Hinshelwood rate | $r = \dfrac{k \cdot K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}$ | $k$ = rate constant, $K$ = adsorption equilibrium constant | AOP modeling | | 5 | Adsorption capacity | $q_e = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | mg/g | CNT, GO batch studies | | 6 | Removal efficiency | $\%R = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0} \times 100$ | % | All nano/bio processes | | 7 | Photon energy | $E = hc / \lambda$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | UV source selection | | 8 | TiO₂ cut-off wavelength | $\lambda_{\max} = 1240 / E_g$ | nm (when $E_g$ in eV) | Solar vs UV-A lamp | | 9 | Bioaccumulation factor | $\text{BAF} = C_{\text{plant}} / C_{\text{soil}}$ | dimensionless | Phytoremediation | | 10 | Monod growth kinetics | $\mu = \mu_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate, $S$ = substrate | Bioreactor design | | 11 | Biomass yield | $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g biomass / g substrate | Stoichiometry | | 12 | Stoichiometry — aerobic | $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y\text{O}_z + (x + y/4 - z/2)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | moles | Oxygen demand | > [!NOTE] > **Never write absolute value** $C_0 - C_e$ with the keyboard pipe symbol `|` in your exam sheet — KTU's OCR-based answer sheets can confuse it with the digit 1. Use parentheses or absolute phrasing like "difference between initial and equilibrium concentrations." ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bioremediation** is the *first-choice* technology for **oil-spill cleanup** (Exxon Valdez, Deepwater Horizon), **landfill leachate**, and **pulp-paper mill effluents** because it leaves no chemical residue. - **Nanofiltration (NF)** and **reverse osmosis (RO)** membranes with nano-engineered pores are deployed in the **World's largest desalination plants** (Sorek, Israel — 624,000 m³/day). - **TiO₂ photocatalysis** is the basis of **self-cleaning concrete** (TX Active®, Italcementi) and **air-purifying paints** (STO Color). - **nZVI slurry injection** is a commercial in-situ remediation technology for **chlorinated-solvent plumes** under buildings (e.g., REGENESIS® NanoIron). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Langmuir–Hinshelwood Photocatalytic Kinetics The **Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L–H)** model is the most cited kinetic model in photocatalysis research. We derive it from first principles. **Step 1 — Surface adsorption equilibrium (Langmuir assumption):** A pollutant molecule $C$ adsorbs onto a vacant catalyst site $S$ to form an adsorbed complex $C\cdot S$, characterized by an equilibrium constant $K$ (in L/mg): $$C + S \;\overset{K}{\rightleftharpoons}\; C\cdot S$$ The fractional surface coverage $\theta$ is: $$\theta = \frac{K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}$$ **Step 2 — Rate-determining surface reaction:** The rate $r$ is proportional to the fraction of surface covered $\theta$, multiplied by the rate constant $k_r$ (in mg/L·min): $$r = k_r \cdot \theta = k_r \cdot \frac{K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}$$ **Step 3 — Pseudo-first-order limit (low concentration, $KC \ll 1$):** When the pollutant concentration is low (e.g., $C < 5\ \text{mg/L}$), the term $KC$ in the denominator is much smaller than 1. We can apply the limit $1 + KC \approx 1$: $$r \approx k_r \cdot K \cdot C = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot C$$ where $k_{\text{obs}} = k_r \cdot K$ is the **apparent pseudo-first-order rate constant**. **Step 4 — Integration to first-order form:** For a batch reactor: $$- \frac{dC}{dt} = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot C$$ Separating variables and integrating from $C_0$ to $C_t$: $$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \frac{dC}{C} = - k_{\text{obs}} \int_0^t dt$$ This gives the **linear form used in KTU problems**: $$\ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Half-life derivation:** At $t = t_{1/2}$, $C_t = C_0/2$: $$\ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C_0/2}\right) = \ln 2 = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t_{1/2}$$ Therefore: $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{0.693}{k_{\text{obs}}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Surface-Area-to-Volume Ratio for a Spherical Nanoparticle **Step 1 — Surface area of a sphere:** $$SA = 4 \pi r^2$$ **Step 2 — Volume of a sphere:** $$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio:** $$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{4 \pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r^3}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel common terms:** $$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{4 \pi r^2 \cdot 3}{4 \pi r^3} = \frac{3 \cdot r^2}{r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$ **Numerical comparison (KTU favorite):** For a 1 µm microparticle ($r = 0.5\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}$): $$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{3}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 6 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ For a 10 nm nanoparticle ($r = 5\ \text{nm} = 5 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$): $$\frac{SA}{V} = \frac{3}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 6 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m}^{-1}$$ Ratio: nanoparticle SA/V is **100× greater** than microparticle SA/V. ## 3.3 Analytical Numerical Example — Full KTU Solution > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem (typical 7-mark question):** > A photocatalytic reactor uses TiO₂ nanoparticles to degrade a textile dye. The initial dye concentration is **50 mg/L**. After **60 minutes** of UV-A irradiation, the residual concentration is **12.5 mg/L**. Assuming **pseudo-first-order kinetics**, calculate: > (a) The rate constant $k_{\text{obs}}$ > (b) The half-life $t_{1/2}$ > (c) The time required to achieve **95% removal** (i.e., $C_t = 0.05 \cdot C_0$) **Solution:** **(a) Rate constant:** Using $\ln(C_0/C_t) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t$: $$\ln\left(\frac{50}{12.5}\right) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot 60$$ $$\ln(4) = 60 \cdot k_{\text{obs}}$$ $$1.3863 = 60 \cdot k_{\text{obs}}$$ $$k_{\text{obs}} = \frac{1.3863}{60} = 0.0231\ \text{min}^{-1}$$ **(b) Half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{0.693}{0.0231} = 30\ \text{min}$$ **(c) Time for 95% removal:** At 95% removal, $C_t = 0.05 \cdot C_0$, so $C_0 / C_t = 20$: $$\ln(20) = 0.0231 \cdot t$$ $$2.9957 = 0.0231 \cdot t$$ $$t = \frac{2.9957}{0.0231} = 129.7\ \text{min} \approx 130\ \text{min}$$ **Verification:** $130 \approx 4.3 \times t_{1/2}$, consistent with $C/C_0 = 0.05$ (≈ $2^{-4.32}$). ✓ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Process Selection | Criterion | Bioremediation | Photocatalysis (TiO₂) | nZVI Reduction | Phytoremediation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Target | Organic, biodegradable | Refractory organics | Chlorinated, Cr(VI) | Heavy metals, organics | | Time scale | Weeks–months | Hours–days | Days–weeks | Months–years | | By-products | CO₂ + biomass (clean) | CO₂ + H₂O (clean) | Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ ions | Harvested biomass | | Cost | **Low** | High (UV source) | Moderate | **Low** | | Limitation | Toxicity to microbes | Recombination, band gap | Passivation, agglomeration | Slow, climate-dependent | | KTU CO mapping | CO2, CO3 | CO3, CO4 | CO4 | CO2 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Integrated Bio–Nano Pollution Control Train ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Influent Wastewater
Industrial or Municipal] --> B[Screen and Grit Chamber
Physical Primary] B --> C[Equalization Basin
Flow and Load Buffering] C --> D[Bio-Reactor Unit
Aerobic or Anaerobic Microbial Degradation] D --> E[Secondary Clarifier
Biomass Separation] E --> F[Nano-Filtration Membrane
Pore Size 0.5 to 2 nm] F --> G[Photocatalytic Polishing
TiO2 plus UV A Reactor] G --> H[Discharge to Environment
Meets KTU Regulatory Limit] E --> I[Sludge Thickener
Biosolids Handling] I --> J[Anaerobic Digester
Biogas Production] J --> K[CH4 and CO2 Capture
Energy Recovery] F --> L[Reject Brine
Reverse Osmosis Recovery] L --> F style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style D fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style E fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style F fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style G fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff style H fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style I fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style J fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style K fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style L fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Photocatalysis Mechanism on TiO₂ Nanoparticle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Photon h nu
Energy greater than 3.2 eV] --> B[TiO2 Nanoparticle
Anatase Phase] B --> C[Conduction Band e-] B --> D[Valence Band h+] C --> E[Oxygen Reduction
O2 plus e- gives O2 minus radical] D --> F[Water Oxidation
H2O plus h+ gives OH radical] F --> G[Adsorbed Dye Molecule R] E --> G G --> H[Intermediate Radicals] H --> I[CO2 plus H2O plus Mineral Acids] style A fill:#fde68a,color:#000000 style B fill:#334155,color:#ffffff style C fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff style D fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style E fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style F fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff style G fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style H fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style I fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Bioremediation Decision Flow (Biostimulation vs Bioaugmentation) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Site Assessment
Soil and Water Sampling] --> B{Indigenous
Microbes Present?} B -- Yes --> C{Acceptable
Degradation Rate?} B -- No --> D[Bioaugmentation
Add Specialized Consortia] C -- No --> E[Biostimulation
Add Nutrients N P K
Adjust pH and Eh
Add Electron Acceptor] C -- Yes --> F[Monitor Only
Natural Attenuation] D --> G[Bioreactor or In Situ Injection] E --> G F --> H[Long Term Monitoring
Quarterly Sampling] G --> I[Endpoint Analysis
Meet Regulatory Closure] H --> I style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style B fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff style C fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff style D fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style E fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style F fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style G fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style H fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff style I fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Set] **Define photocatalysis. With a neat sketch, explain the working of TiO₂-mediated photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants.** **Model Answer:** Photocatalysis is the acceleration of a photoreaction in the presence of a **semiconductor catalyst** (e.g., TiO₂, ZnO, WO₃). When TiO₂ (band-gap energy = **3.2 eV** for anatase) absorbs a UV photon of wavelength $\lambda \leq 388\ \text{nm}$, an electron from the valence band (VB) is promoted to the conduction band (CB), generating an **electron–hole pair**: $$\text{TiO}_2 + h\nu \longrightarrow \text{e}^-_{CB} + \text{h}^+_{VB}$$ The hole oxidizes adsorbed water to produce the highly reactive **hydroxyl radical (·OH)**: $$\text{h}^+_{VB} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{H}^+$$ The conduction-band electron reduces O₂ to superoxide: $$\text{e}^-_{CB} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{O}_2^{\cdot -}$$ Both ·OH and O₂·⁻ are powerful oxidants that **non-selectively mineralize** organic pollutants (R) to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral acids. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set] **Differentiate between bioaugmentation and biostimulation with one suitable example each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | **Bioaugmentation** | **Biostimulation** | |---|---|---| | Definition | Adding **exogenous microbial cultures** to the contaminated site | **Modifying environmental conditions** (nutrients, pH, electron acceptors) to enhance native microbial activity | | When applied | Site lacks competent indigenous degraders | Site already has capable microbes but activity is limited by nutrients or conditions | | Example | Adding *Pseudomonas putida* to a TCE-contaminated aquifer | Adding N-P-K fertilizer and oxygen to an oil-spill beach to stimulate *Alcanivorax borkumensis* | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Model Set] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **mechanism of microbial degradation of petroleum hydrocarbons** with a focus on aerobic pathways. Discuss the role of **biosurfactants** in enhancing bioavailability and provide the **electron acceptor hierarchy**. **(b) [7 Marks]** A batch photocatalytic reactor treats **5 L** of dye-laden wastewater with initial concentration **120 mg/L**. After **45 minutes** under UV-A/TiO₂, the concentration drops to **30 mg/L**. Assuming **pseudo-first-order kinetics**, calculate: **(i)** the rate constant $k_{\text{obs}}$ in min⁻¹, **(ii)** the half-life $t_{1/2}$, and **(iii)** the time for **98% removal**. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (a) **Mechanism of Aerobic Hydrocarbon Degradation:** 1. **Uptake of n-alkanes** — *Alcanivorax* and *Pseudomonas* species use *alkB* gene-encoded **alkane monooxygenase** to convert alkanes to primary alcohols. 2. **Oxidation cascade:** primary alcohol → aldehyde → fatty acid via alcohol dehydrogenase and aldehyde dehydrogenase. 3. **β-oxidation** in the cytoplasm — fatty acids are cleaved into acetyl-CoA units in the TCA cycle. 4. **Aromatic ring cleavage** — *Pseudomonas* uses *tod* and *cat* pathway dioxygenases (catechol 1,2-dioxygenase) for ortho- or meta-cleavage. 5. **Final mineralization:** CO₂ + H₂O + new biomass. **Role of Biosurfactants:** Hydrocarbons are hydrophobic (log $K_{ow} > 4$). Microbes secrete **rhamnolipids**, **sophorolipids**, or **surfactin** which: - Reduce **interfacial tension** from ~30 mN/m to < 1 mN/m. - Form **micelles** with hydrocarbon cores, increasing apparent aqueous solubility by 100–1000×. - Enhance **bioavailability** and mass-transfer rate. **Electron Acceptor Hierarchy (Thermodynamic preference, high to low energy yield):** $$\text{O}_2 \;>\; \text{NO}_3^- \;>\; \text{Mn}^{4+} \;>\; \text{Fe}^{3+} \;>\; \text{SO}_4^{2-} \;>\; \text{CO}_2$$ Engineers add **electron acceptors** (O₂ via aeration; NO₃⁻ via nitrate injection) to push the system into the most favorable pathway. --- ### Model Solution — Question A (b) **Given:** $C_0 = 120\ \text{mg/L}$, $C_t = 30\ \text{mg/L}$, $t = 45\ \text{min}$, $V = 5\ \text{L}$ (volume not needed for kinetic calculation). **(i) Rate constant:** $$\ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C_t}\right) = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot t$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{120}{30}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.3863 = k_{\text{obs}} \cdot 45$$ $$k_{\text{obs}} = \frac{1.3863}{45} = 0.0308\ \text{min}^{-1}$$ **(ii) Half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k_{\text{obs}}} = \frac{0.693}{0.0308} = 22.5\ \text{min}$$ **(iii) Time for 98% removal** (i.e., $C_t = 0.02 \cdot C_0 = 2.4\ \text{mg/L}$, so $C_0/C_t = 50$): $$\ln(50) = 0.0308 \cdot t$$ $$3.912 = 0.0308 \cdot t$$ $$t = \frac{3.912}{0.0308} = 127.0\ \text{min}$$ **Answer:** $k_{\text{obs}} = 0.0308\ \text{min}^{-1}$, $t_{1/2} = 22.5\ \text{min}$, $t_{98\%} = 127\ \text{min}$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Alternative Set] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **synthesis, properties, and environmental applications of nano-zero-valent iron (nZVI)**. With balanced chemical equations, show how nZVI remediates **trichloroethylene (TCE)** and **hexavalent chromium Cr(VI)**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **mechanism of phytoremediation** with a labeled block diagram. Differentiate between **phytoextraction, phytodegradation, and phytovolatilization** and list **two suitable plant species** for each. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (a) **Synthesis of nZVI:** 1. **Borohydride reduction (most common):** $$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{BH}_4^- + 6\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Fe}^0 \downarrow + 2\text{B(OH)}_3 + 7\text{H}_2 \uparrow$$ 2. **Green synthesis** (using plant extracts as reducing agents — *Camellia sinensis*, eucalyptus leaf) — produces nZVI of 10–100 nm. 3. **Thermal decomposition** of Fe(CO)₅ in surfactant media. **Properties:** - Particle size: **10–100 nm**; high specific surface area **20–50 m²/g**. - Core–shell structure: Fe⁰ core with Fe₃O₄/FeOOH shell. - **Superparamagnetic** when below 20 nm. - Strong reducing agent: $E^0(\text{Fe}^{2+}/\text{Fe}^0) = -0.44\ \text{V}$. **Environmental Applications:** chlorinated solvent DNAPL plumes, Cr(VI) reduction, As(III) oxidation coupled with adsorption, heavy-metal removal. **Reactions:** For TCE (β-elimination pathway): $$\text{C}_2\text{HCl}_3 + 3\text{Fe}^0 + 3\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + 3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 3\text{Cl}^-$$ For Cr(VI) reduction (in acidic groundwater): $$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7^{2-} + 2\text{Fe}^0 + 14\text{H}^+ \longrightarrow 2\text{Cr}^{3+} + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 7\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ The Cr³⁺ precipitates as Cr(OH)₃ at pH > 5.5, achieving **immobilization**. --- ### Model Solution — Question B (b) **Phytoremediation Mechanism:** Plants uptake, accumulate, translocate, sequester, or transform contaminants via their root–shoot systems. The process is divided into: | Sub-process | Mechanism | Plant Examples | |---|---|---| | **Phytoextraction** | Roots absorb metals → translocate to shoots → harvest shoot biomass | *Brassica juncea* (Indian mustard) for Pb; *Arabidopsis halleri* for Cd/Zn | | **Phytodegradation** | Plant-derived enzymes (dehalogenase, nitroreductase, laccase) cleave organics | *Populus* sp. for TCE; *Myriophyllum spicatum* for atrazine | | **Phytovolatilization** | Contaminant converted to volatile form and released through stomata | *Brassica juncea* for Se → dimethyl selenide; transgenic *Arabidopsis* for Hg → Hg⁰ | | **Phytostabilization** | Roots adsorb/precipitate contaminants, preventing leaching | *Agrostis tenuis* for As; *Festuca arundinacea* for Pb | | **Rhizofiltration** | Roots in solution adsorb dissolved metals | *Sunflower* (*Helianthus annuus*) for Cs, Sr, Cr; *Pteris vittata* (Chinese brake fern) for As | **Block diagram (to be drawn in the answer sheet):** - Block 1: Contaminant source - Block 2: Root zone uptake - Block 3: Translocation (xylem) - Block 4: Leaf/shoot accumulation or volatilization - Block 5: Harvest and disposal (for phytoextraction) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss:** > 1. **Confusing bioaugmentation vs biostimulation** — examiners specifically test this. Memorize the table. > 2. **Wrong band-gap of TiO₂** — it is **3.2 eV (anatase)**, not 3.0 eV (rutile). Wrong value = −1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting to balance chemical equations** for nZVI reactions. Always show stoichiometric coefficients. > 4. **Writing $C_0 - C_e$ as $C_0/C_e$** in adsorption capacity formula — this is a recurring error. > 5. **Missing the half-life derivation** — KTU often asks "show that $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k$" — derive, don't quote. > 6. **Using unit "min⁻¹" for $k$** without showing dimensional analysis — examiners look for unit clarity. > 7. **Confusing *phytoextraction* with *phytostabilization*** — students mix up "uptake" and "immobilization." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:** - [ ] **Biotechnology** = use of *biological agents* (microbes, plants, enzymes) for pollutant removal. *Bioremediation* is the operative engineering term. - [ ] **Microbial pathways** are *aerobic* (O₂ acceptor, fast) or *anaerobic* (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₂ acceptors, slower but capable of dechlorination). - [ ] **Bioaugmentation** = add microbes. **Biostimulation** = feed native microbes. - [ ] **Phytoremediation** requires **BAF > 1** and **TF > 1** for hyperaccumulator status. - [ ] **Nanotechnology** exploits *1–100 nm* scale → SA/V scales as **3/r**. - [ ] **TiO₂ band gap = 3.2 eV** (anatase) → activated by $\lambda \leq 388\ \text{nm}$ (UV-A). - [ ] **Photocatalysis** generates **·OH** (redox potential +2.80 V) and **O₂·⁻** that mineralize organics. - [ ] **Pseudo-first-order:** $\ln(C_0/C_t) = k_{\text{obs}} t$; **half-life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$. - [ ] **Langmuir–Hinshelwood** is the standard kinetic model; at low $C$, it reduces to pseudo-first-order. - [ ] **nZVI** is Fe⁰ (10–100 nm, surface area 20–50 m²/g); reduces TCE to ethene and Cr(VI) to Cr(III). - [ ] **CNT and GO** are excellent *adsorbents* for dyes, heavy metals; functionalization (−COOH, −OH) enhances capacity. - [ ] **Magnetic nanoparticles (Fe₃O₄)** allow *targeted recovery* using external magnets. - [ ] **Photon energy:** $E = hc/\lambda$; **cut-off wavelength** for a semiconductor: $\lambda_{\max} = 1240/E_g$ (in nm, with $E_g$ in eV). - [ ] **Integrated train** = bio-process (bulk removal) → nano-membrane (refining) → photocatalysis (polishing). - [ ] **Sustainable advantage:** Bio + Nano systems produce **CO₂, H₂O, and reusable biomass** as by-products — minimal secondary waste.

topics.title: Threats and pollution caused by renewable energy technologies (PV cells and batteries)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBBT613 - PROCESS DESIGN FOR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Threats and pollution caused by renewable energy technologies (PV cells and batteries)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Renewable energy technologies** are systems that convert naturally replenishing energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal) into usable electrical, thermal, or mechanical energy. While classified as *clean energy* during the **operational phase**, their **life-cycle impacts** — spanning raw material extraction, manufacturing, deployment, operation, and end-of-life disposal — generate significant environmental burdens.

**Photovoltaic (PV) cells** are solid-state semiconductor devices that convert incident solar radiation directly into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect** (typically using crystalline silicon, CdTe, CIGS, or perovskite absorbers).

**Batteries** are electrochemical energy storage devices that store and release electrical energy through reversible redox reactions. For renewable integration, the dominant chemistries are **lithium-ion (LiB)**, **lead-acid**, **sodium-sulfur (NaS)**, **vanadium redox flow (VRFB)**, and emerging **solid-state** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Green Energy ≠ Pollution-Free"**: The pollution burden of renewables is *upstream* (mining, refining) and *downstream* (waste), not *operational* (during electricity generation). This is the central paradox the KTU module tests.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Iceberg Analogy for Renewable Pollution**
>
> Imagine a PV solar panel as an iceberg floating in the ocean. The visible tip (the operational phase — silent, emission-free, 25–30 years of clean electricity) looks pristine. But **90% of the iceberg's mass lies beneath the surface** — this is the *embodied* pollution: the silicon quarries in China, the hydrofluoric acid baths in Malaysia, the cobalt mines in the DRC, and the un-recycled panels piling up in Kasauli, India. The same applies to lithium-ion batteries — the EV on the road looks clean, but the lithium brine pools in the Atacama Desert, the lithium-ion battery fires in shipping containers, and the lead smelting in recycling yards are all *invisible environmental costs*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Crystalline silicon bandgap** $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$
- **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$
- **AM1.5 standard spectrum** peak irradiance = **1000 W/m²**
- **TCLP (Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure) regulatory threshold for lead** = **5 mg/L**
- **TCLP threshold for cadmium** = **1 mg/L**
- **Energy Payback Time (EPBT)** for modern c-Si PV = **1.5 – 2.5 years**
- **Lithium-ion thermal runaway onset temperature** ≈ **80 – 120 °C**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly asks students to critically evaluate the *net environmental benefit* of renewables by accounting for life-cycle emissions, hazardous material handling, and end-of-life management.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pollution Inventory Across the PV Life-Cycle

**Stage 1 — Raw Material Extraction & Refining (Most Polluting)**
- **High-purity quartz mining** for silicon feedstock.
- **Energy-intensive polysilicon production** via the **Siemens process**.
- **Heavy metal extraction** for thin-film technologies (Cd, Te, In, Ga, Se).

**Stage 2 — Cell & Module Manufacturing**
- Wet-chemical etching using **HF (hydrofluoric acid)**, **HCl**, **HNO₃**, **NaOH**.
- Surface texturing with **KOH/IPA solutions**.
- **PECVD deposition** of SiNₓ using **NH₃** and **SiH₄** (silane) — both toxic/pyrophoric.
- Screen-printing of silver and aluminum pastes (Ag⁺ and Al³⁺ wastewater).
- Lead-bearing solder (SnPb) for ribbon interconnection.

**Stage 3 — Operation**
- Direct emissions ≈ **zero**.
- Indirect: land-use change, albedo modification.

**Stage 4 — End-of-Life (E-Waste Stream)**
- **30+ year life** means first-generation waste is arriving *now*.
- IEA estimates **78 million tonnes** of PV waste by 2050.
- Leaching of Pb, Cd, Se, Ga into **soil → groundwater → food chain**.

### 2.2 Pollution Inventory Across the Battery Life-Cycle

**Stage 1 — Critical Mineral Extraction**
- **Lithium**: Brine extraction (16,000–22,000 L water per ton Li) or hard-rock spodumene mining.
- **Cobalt**: Often from *Cobalt Blue Belt* (DRC) — acid mine drainage (AMD), child labor concerns.
- **Nickel**: Laterite (saprolite) ore; produces chromium-laden waste.
- **Graphite**: Carbon refinement.

**Stage 2 — Cell Manufacturing**
- **NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone)** as PVDF binder solvent — a reproductive toxin.
- **Electrolyte salts**: **LiPF₆** → hydrolyzes to **HF** on contact with moisture.
- **Carbonate solvents** (EC, DMC, EMC, DEC) — volatile, flammable.
- **Cleanroom fabrication** — massive energy load (low-carbon footprint *only* in clean grids).

**Stage 3 — Operation**
- Zero direct emissions.
- Degradation: capacity fade, swelling, dendrite formation.

**Stage 4 — End-of-Life**
- **Thermal runaway** → fires, venting of **HF, CO, CO₂, C₂H₄, CH₄**.
- **Shredding dust** — fine particulate (Li, Co, Ni, Mn oxides) — **occupational hazard**.
- Recycling rate globally ≈ **< 5%** for LiBs.

### 2.3 Why "Green" is a Misnomer — the "Hidden Carbon" Argument

- **Embodied energy** of c-Si PV ≈ **400 – 600 kWh/m²**.
- **Manufacture of 1 kg polysilicon** generates ≈ **25–35 kg CO₂-eq**.
- The **carbon payback time** is short only if the panel is installed in high-irradiance regions (Rajasthan, Saudi Arabia) — longer in cloudy climates (Kerala, Northern Europe).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Expression / Value | Units |
|--------|------------|--------------------|-------|
| $EPBT$ | Energy Payback Time | $EPBT = \dfrac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen,annual}}$ | years |
| $E_{LC}$ | Life-cycle primary energy demand | $E_{LC} = E_{manuf} + E_{inst} + E_{EoL}$ | kWh |
| $E_{gen,annual}$ | Annual electrical output | $\eta_{sys} \cdot H \cdot A$ | kWh/yr |
| $\eta_{sys}$ | System (overall) efficiency | $\eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{BOS}$ | dimensionless |
| $H$ | Annual avg. global horizontal irradiance (GHI) | 1500–2200 (India) | kWh/m²/yr |
| $A$ | Module area | — | m² |
| $CPBT$ | Carbon Payback Time | $CPBT = \dfrac{CO_{2,LC}}{CO_{2,avoided,annual}}$ | years |
| $CO_{2,avoided}$ | Annual CO₂ displaced | $E_{gen,annual} \cdot EF_{grid}$ | kg CO₂/yr |
| $EF_{grid}$ | Grid emission factor | 0.7 (India 2022 avg.) | kg CO₂/kWh |
| $LCA$ | Life-Cycle Assessment boundary | Cradle-to-Gate or Cradle-to-Grave | — |
| $TCLP$ | Toxicity Charact. Leaching Procedure | $C_{leachate}$ vs. $C_{threshold}$ | mg/L |
| $C_{lim, Pb}$ | Lead leaching limit | **5** | mg/L |
| $C_{lim, Cd}$ | Cadmium leaching limit | **1** | mg/L |
| $T_{TR}$ | Thermal runaway onset | 80–120 | °C |
| $\Delta H_{rxn}$ | Enthalpy of reaction (Siemens) | ≈ 178 (SiHCl₃ formation) | kJ/mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** These equations allow a process engineer to size a PV/battery installation while simultaneously computing its **net environmental footprint**. They are the foundation of any **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** submitted to India's **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)**.

### 2.5 Real-World Industrial Relevance

- **Solar farms in Rajasthan** (Bhadla, Pavagada) require **land-use compensation** — ecological trade-off.
- **EV battery fires in shipping** (e.g., MV Fremantle Highway 2023) — **HF plume** threatened fire crews.
- **First Solar's CdTe recycling program** recovers **95% Cd, 90% Te** — a benchmark for circular design.
- **India's E-Waste Management Rules 2022** — extended producer responsibility (EPR) for PV and battery manufacturers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Payback Time (EPBT) for a c-Si PV Module

**Given system parameters** (standard KTU textbook-style problem):

- Module area $A = 1.6 \text{ m}^2$ (typical 400 Wp c-Si panel)
- Module efficiency $\eta_{PV} = 20\%$
- Inverter efficiency $\eta_{inv} = 96\%$
- Balance-of-system (BOS) efficiency $\eta_{BOS} = 90\%$
- Annual GHI at site $H = 1800 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$ (e.g., Rajasthan)
- Embodied energy $E_{LC} = 850 \text{ kWh/m}^2$ (cradle-to-gate average)

**Step 1 — Compute system efficiency**

$$
\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{BOS}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\eta_{sys} = 0.20 \times 0.96 \times 0.90 = 0.1728
$$

**Step 2 — Compute annual electrical generation per unit installed area**

$$
E_{gen,annual} = \eta_{sys} \cdot H
$$

$$
E_{gen,annual} = 0.1728 \times 1800 = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute EPBT**

$$
EPBT = \frac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen,annual}}
$$

$$
EPBT = \frac{850}{311.04} = 2.73 \text{ years}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** With a 30-year service life, the panel delivers ≈ **27.3 years of net clean energy** — a positive return. However, the 2.73 years of polluting energy must be sourced responsibly (e.g., hydropower for polysilicon refining in Yunnan, China).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometry of Polysilicon Production (Siemens Process)

The Siemens process produces high-purity electronic-grade silicon. We analyze the **toxic byproduct** — silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄) — which is the *single largest atmospheric pollutant* in PV manufacturing.

**Overall reactions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reaction 1 (Carbothermic reduction of silica):} \quad \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{C} &\rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\text{CO} \\
\text{Reaction 2 (Hydrochlorination of metallurgical Si):} \quad \text{Si} + 3\text{HCl} &\rightarrow \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 \\
\text{Reaction 3 (Siemens CVD deposition):} \quad \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{H}_2 &\rightarrow \text{Si} + 3\text{HCl} \\
\text{Side reaction (chlorination of impurity Si):} \quad \text{Si} + 4\text{HCl} &\rightarrow \text{SiCl}_4 + 2\text{H}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric ratio for trichlorosilane**

From Reaction 2, **1 mol Si → 1 mol SiHCl₃**. Assume 1000 kg of metallurgical-grade Si (MGS) is fed and 70% reacts via Reaction 2 and 30% via Reaction 4.

**Step 2 — Mass of Si converted to SiHCl₃**

$$
m_{Si, TCS} = 1000 \times 0.70 = 700 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of SiHCl₃ produced**

Molar mass of Si = 28.09 g/mol:

$$
n_{SiHCl_3} = \frac{700{,}000 \text{ g}}{28.09 \text{ g/mol}} = 24{,}922 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of SiHCl₃**

Molar mass of SiHCl₃ = 28.09 + 1.008 + 3(35.45) = 135.45 g/mol:

$$
m_{SiHCl_3} = 24{,}922 \times 135.45 = 3.376 \times 10^6 \text{ g} = 3376 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass of HCl consumed (Reaction 2, 3:1 molar ratio)**

$$
n_{HCl} = 3 \times 24{,}922 = 74{,}766 \text{ mol}
$$

Molar mass HCl = 36.46 g/mol:

$$
m_{HCl} = 74{,}766 \times 36.46 = 2726 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of SiCl₄ byproduct (Reaction 4, 1:1 with Si)**

$$
m_{Si, SiCl_4} = 1000 \times 0.30 = 300 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
n_{SiCl_4} = \frac{300{,}000}{28.09} = 10{,}679 \text{ mol}
$$

Molar mass SiCl₄ = 28.09 + 4(35.45) = 169.89 g/mol:

$$
m_{SiCl_4} = 10{,}679 \times 169.89 = 1814 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 7 — HCl recovered from Reaction 3**

For every mole of SiHCl₃ that *deposits* as Si (CVD), 3 mol HCl is regenerated. If 80% of the SiHCl₃ from Step 3 is sent to the CVD reactor:

$$
n_{HCl, recovered} = 0.80 \times 3 \times 24{,}922 = 59{,}813 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
m_{HCl, recovered} = 59{,}813 \times 36.46 = 2181 \text{ kg}
$$

**Net HCl consumption:**

$$
m_{HCl, net} = 2726 - 2181 = 545 \text{ kg HCl per 1000 kg MGS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental consequence of SiCl₄:** SiCl₄ is hydrolyzed to **HCl (acid rain precursor)** and **SiO₂ (fine particulate)** upon atmospheric exposure. Uncontrolled release at PV manufacturing hubs in Xinjiang caused documented respiratory disease clusters. Modern plants run a *closed-loop* plasma converter: $\text{SiCl}_4 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiHCl}_3 + \text{HCl}$, eliminating 95% of the waste.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carbon Payback Time (CPBT) for PV vs. Indian Coal Grid

**Given:**

- Indian grid emission factor $EF_{grid} = 0.70 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$
- Panel area $A_{total} = 1000 \text{ m}^2$ (small solar farm)
- $E_{gen,annual} = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}$ (from §3.1)
- Life-cycle GHG emission of panel $GHG_{LC} = 40 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Total annual generation**

$$
E_{gen,total} = 311.04 \times 1000 = 311{,}040 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual CO₂ avoided**

$$
CO_{2,avoided} = 311{,}040 \times 0.70 = 217{,}728 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 — Total life-cycle emissions**

$$
GHG_{total} = 40 \times 1000 = 40{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4 — CPBT**

$$
CPBT = \frac{GHG_{total}}{CO_{2,avoided}} = \frac{40{,}000}{217{,}728} = 0.184 \text{ years} \approx 67 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The CPBT for PV in India is **dramatically shorter** than the EPBT because Indian coal is so carbon-intensive. This is precisely why India has a *strategic* (not just environmental) interest in scaling PV — geopolitical energy security.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cobalt Demand for Grid-Scale LiB Storage

**Given:**

- Target: 100 MWh grid storage
- NMC 622 cathode chemistry: Li(Ni₀.₆Mn₀.₂Co₀.₂)O₂
- Average specific energy: 180 Wh/kg (cell level)
- Co mass fraction in NMC 622: 0.20 (molar) × 58.93 g/mol

**Step 1 — Total mass of cells required**

$$
m_{cell} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{180 \text{ Wh/kg}} = 555{,}556 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Cathode is ≈ 30% of cell mass** (typical)

$$
m_{cathode} = 0.30 \times 555{,}556 = 166{,}667 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Active material is ≈ 90% of cathode**

$$
m_{NMC} = 0.90 \times 166{,}667 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Cobalt mass**

$$
m_{Co} = 0.20 \times 150{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg} = 30 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — At DRC ore grade of 0.3% Co and 50% recovery:**

$$
\text{Ore tonnage required} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.003 \times 0.5} = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg} = 20{,}000 \text{ tonnes of ore}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environmental implication:** Just 100 MWh of grid storage requires **20,000 tonnes of ore**, with proportional AMD, water consumption, and tailings. Scaling to GWh is environmentally non-trivial — this drives R&D into **LFP (LiFePO₄)** and **sodium-ion** chemistries, which eliminate Co entirely.
