## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three toxic materials used in the manufacture of crystalline silicon photovoltaic cells and state one environmental hazard associated with each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Hydrofluoric acid (HF)** used for surface texturing and edge isolation — acute toxicity, causes severe burns and pulmonary edema; lethal at concentrations as low as 100 ppm inhaled.
2. **Silicon tetrachloride (SiCl₄)** — byproduct of the Siemens process; atmospheric hydrolysis produces **HCl** (acid rain precursor) and fine **SiO₂** particulate (respiratory hazard).
3. **Lead (Pb) in solder** (SnPb ribbon interconnect) — at end-of-life, TCLP leachate exceeds 5 mg/L threshold, contaminating groundwater with neurotoxin (especially harmful to children).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain why lithium-ion batteries, despite enabling renewable energy storage, are considered a pollution concern.*

**Model Answer:**

Lithium-ion batteries are a pollution concern because:

- **Critical mineral extraction** (Li, Co, Ni) generates acid mine drainage, consumes massive water (≈ 16,000 L water per ton Li from brine), and damages ecosystems (e.g., Salar de Atacama, DRC cobalt belt).
- **Electrolyte salt LiPF₆** hydrolyzes to **HF** on moisture contact; venting during thermal runaway releases HF, CO, and flammable hydrocarbon gases (EC, DMC decomposition products).
- **End-of-life recycling rate is < 5% globally**, leading to landfill accumulation of Li, Co, Ni, Mn — all leachable heavy metals.
- **Occupational hazards** during shredding — fine particulate of transition metal oxides is a respiratory and carcinogenic risk.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module 4 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block flow diagram, describe the various sources of pollution across the life-cycle stages of a crystalline silicon photovoltaic module. Identify the most polluting stage and justify.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1.6 m² c-Si PV module has a system efficiency of 17.28% and is installed at a site with annual GHI of 1800 kWh/m²/yr. The cradle-to-gate embodied energy is 850 kWh/m². Compute the **energy payback time (EPBT)** and the **carbon payback time (CPBT)** assuming the Indian grid emission factor is 0.70 kg CO₂/kWh and life-cycle GHG emissions are 40 kg CO₂-eq/m².

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pollution sources across the PV life-cycle:**

| Life-Cycle Stage | Pollutant | Pathway |
|------------------|-----------|---------|
| Quartz mining | Land disturbance, dust | Habitat loss |
| Polysilicon (Siemens) | SiCl₄, HCl, CO, SiH₄ | Air/soil |
| Wafer slicing | SiC slurry, IPA, glycol | Wastewater |
| Cell fabrication | HF, HCl, HNO₃, NaOH, NH₃ | Wastewater/air |
| Module assembly | Pb solder, EVA off-gassing | Solid waste |
| EOL landfill | Pb, Cd, Se leaching | Groundwater |

**Most polluting stage:** *Polysilicon (Siemens) production* — energy intensive (>100 kWh/kg), emits SiCl₄ (a globally significant chemical waste), CO from carbothermic reduction, and is responsible for **~ 60% of the panel's embodied energy**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

System efficiency $\eta_{sys} = 17.28\% = 0.1728$

Annual generation per m²:

$$
E_{gen} = 0.1728 \times 1800 = 311.04 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

EPBT:

$$
EPBT = \frac{E_{LC}}{E_{gen}} = \frac{850}{311.04} = 2.73 \text{ years}
$$

Annual CO₂ avoided per m²:

$$
CO_{2,avoided} = 311.04 \times 0.70 = 217.73 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{m}^2/\text{yr}
$$

CPBT:

$$
CPBT = \frac{40}{217.73} = 0.184 \text{ years} \approx 67 \text{ days}
$$

**Result:** EPBT = 2.73 years; CPBT ≈ 67 days. CPBT << EPBT because the avoided CO₂ is calculated on India's coal-heavy grid.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the pollution threats associated with lithium-ion battery manufacturing, deployment, and disposal. Mention specific toxic chemicals and end-of-life risks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 100 MWh grid-scale LiB storage uses NMC 622 cathodes (Co mass fraction 20% in active material, 30% cathode in cell, 90% active material in cathode, specific energy 180 Wh/kg). Calculate (i) total cobalt mass required, and (ii) tonnage of DRC ore (0.3% Co grade, 50% metallurgical recovery) needed. Comment on the environmental implications.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pollution threats of LiB:**

1. **Mineral extraction stage:** Li brine pumping depletes scarce water; Co and Ni sulfide mining generates AMD ($\text{FeS}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{Fe(OH)}_3$).
2. **Cell manufacturing:** NMP solvent (PVDF binder) is a reproductive toxin and VOC; LiPF₆ hydrolyzes to HF; EC/DMC are volatile and flammable.
3. **Deployment:** Thermal runaway at 80–120 °C vents HF, CO, C₂H₄; risk of cascading cell-to-cell propagation in packs.
4. **End-of-life:** < 5% global recycling; shredding produces fine transition-metal oxide dust; landfill leachate contains Li, Co, Ni, Mn.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Total cell mass:

$$
m_{cell} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{180 \text{ Wh/kg}} = 555{,}556 \text{ kg}
$$

Cathode mass:

$$
m_{cathode} = 0.30 \times 555{,}556 = 166{,}667 \text{ kg}
$$

Active NMC mass:

$$
m_{NMC} = 0.90 \times 166{,}667 = 150{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**(i) Cobalt mass:**

$$
m_{Co} = 0.20 \times 150{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg} = 30 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(ii) DRC ore tonnage:**

$$
m_{ore} = \frac{30{,}000}{0.003 \times 0.50} = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ kg} = 20{,}000 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Environmental comment:** Each GWh of NMC-622 storage requires **20,000 tonnes of ore** (per 100 MWh; scale to 20,00,000 tonnes per 10 GWh), with proportional tailings, water use, and AMD generation. This motivates the global shift toward **LFP and Na-ion chemistries** that eliminate cobalt and nickel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in Module 4 (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "operational" and "life-cycle" emissions** — Students often state renewables are "zero-emission" and lose 3–4 marks. The KTU answer key requires explicit *life-cycle* thinking.
> 2. **Forgetting units in EPBT/CPBT calculations** — Always carry the unit *years* in the final answer. A bare numerical value will not get full marks.
> 3. **Omitting the denominator in mass-balance derivations** — When asked about SiCl₄ byproduct, students forget to convert between kg and mol using molar mass. The Siemens-process derivation is a board-favorite — practice it!
> 4. **Ignoring Indian grid context** — Generic "renewables are clean" answers are penalized. The KTU 2024 paper values India-specific discussion (Bhadla solar farm, Kasauli PV waste, Lithium reserves in J&K).
> 5. **Skipping the "mitigation hierarchy"** — When asked about pollution control, always structure your answer as: *Prevent → Reduce → Treat → Recover → Dispose* (in that order).
> 6. **Not defining TCLP** — Examiners expect the full form at first mention. Memorize it.

---

> ✅ **First-generation PV waste** is arriving **now** (panels installed 2000–2010 reaching EOL)
